hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: Portrait of Booker T. Washington, circa 1895
inline_images.caption: Booker T. Washington around 1895, photographed by Frances Benjamin Johnston, the leader whose accommodationist program Du Bois critiqued in The Souls of Black Folk.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (ppmsca.23961)
inline_images.license: Public domain (published before 1929; no known restrictions on publication)

related_chapters.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
related_chapters.why: Du Bois models honest dissent from a dominant leader's program, and McDew recounts publicly refusing to accept nonviolence as a creed at SNCC's founding.

related_chapters.subject: Charles McLaurin
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Grandmother's Books and Booker T. Washington
related_chapters.why: This essay is Du Bois's direct reckoning with Booker T. Washington, whom McLaurin names as a formative influence through a grandmother's books.

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.why: Du Bois insists on the higher education Washington deprioritized, and Terry recounts building a university Afro-American Studies department under a scholarly mentor.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
related_chapters.why: Du Bois prizes the training of leaders and thinkers, and Sellers, a college president, reflects on the movement itself as a university that formed a generation.

related_chapters.subject: Junius Williams
related_chapters.part_title: A Mayoral Race and a Return to Organizing
related_chapters.chapter_title: Your Voice Deserves to Be Heard
related_chapters.why: Du Bois holds that a people must cultivate its own voice and leaders, and Williams describes teaching a new generation that its voice deserves to be heard.

related_chapters.subject: Harold K. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: How Much Did Your Shoes Cost
related_chapters.why: Du Bois engages Washington's economic gospel critically, and Brown weighs an economic program against a generation's values in the same register of debate.

top_voice.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
top_voice.reason: McDew enacts the essay's core move, principled disagreement with a leading figure's prescribed program, by refusing to make nonviolence a lifelong creed at SNCC's founding.

closing_image.alt: Chuck McDew, photographed for The Atlanta Inquirer, June 9, 1962
closing_image.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1931-1963 without copyright renewal)
closing_image.caption: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew, the corpus voice this essay connects to most. McDew enacts the essay's core move, principled disagreement with a leading figure's prescribed program, by refusing to make nonviolence a lifelong creed at SNCC's founding.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of John Dudley et al, Dr. Cleveland Sellers, and Charles McLaurin.
